skill.name: tool-design
skill.description: Guides teams through designing, consolidating, documenting, and evaluating AI agent tools and MCP tool interfaces.
skill.summary: Design clearer agent tools with better names, schemas, descriptions, error handling, and evaluation criteria.
skill.author: ChakshuGautam

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk indicators, but most were false positives caused by Markdown examples, code fences, and ordinary Python string templates. The confirmed concern is instructional guidance that recommends arbitrary bash command execution through a sandbox, which is legitimate for advanced agent architectures but risky if implemented without strict isolation and policy controls.

medium_findings.title: Guidance Encourages Arbitrary Command Tools
medium_findings.description: The skill recommends a reduced architecture with a command execution tool that can run arbitrary bash commands in a sandbox. This is not malware in the skill itself, but implementers could create a serious command execution risk if they omit sandboxing, allow sensitive filesystem access, or pass untrusted input directly to shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command execution guidance is explicit and repeated in the documentation. The risk is medium because the examples require sandboxing and no executable command runner is invoked by the skill itself.

low_findings.title: Bundled Script Has Limited Local Processing Scope
low_findings.description: The included Python script imports only typing helpers and regular expressions, then builds description and error-message templates. No evidence found of network calls, filesystem modification, secret access, subprocess execution, or dynamic code execution in the script.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The inspected script shows local string formatting and regex evaluation only. Static weak-cryptography hits appear to be unrelated text-pattern matches rather than real cryptographic code.

low_findings.title: Network Findings Are Documentation False Positives
low_findings.description: The scanner flagged network-related language, but the cited content discusses agents interacting with the world and parameter naming examples such as url. No evidence found of HTTP requests, external endpoints, credential transfer, or telemetry behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are explanatory prose and naming examples. There is no import or call pattern showing outbound network behavior.

low_findings.title: No Prompt Injection Attempt Found
low_findings.description: The reviewed files do not contain override language, fake system instructions, requests to skip analysis, or claims of pre-approval. No evidence found of prompt injection content in the skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed skill header and body describe tool-design behavior without attempting to override evaluator instructions. Confidence is high because all files named by static analysis were inspected.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary Shell Command Execution Pattern
dangerous_patterns.description: The documentation presents a command execution pattern that accepts a command string and passes it to a sandbox execution API. This pattern must require sandboxing, allowlists, resource limits, and sensitive path isolation before production use.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The shell execution pattern is directly shown in the reference documentation. It is a dangerous pattern when copied without controls, although it is presented as sandboxed guidance.

content.value_statement: Agent tools often fail because names, descriptions, parameters, and errors are ambiguous. This skill provides practical design rules for clearer tool contracts and safer tool collections.
actual_capabilities: Explains how to write tool descriptions that define purpose, usage triggers, inputs, returns, and errors.
Provides guidance for consolidating overlapping tools into clearer workflow-level tools.
Describes MCP naming conventions, including fully qualified tool names.
Shows how to evaluate tool designs for clarity, completeness, recoverability, efficiency, and consistency.
Includes a local Python helper for generating tool descriptions and evaluating description quality.
Documents architectural reduction tradeoffs for advanced agent systems.
limitations: It provides guidance and examples, not a complete production framework.
Command execution examples require careful sandboxing before real use.
The included helper script expects compatible tool specification objects.
It does not validate actual MCP server behavior or permissions.

use_cases.title: Design a New Agent Tool
use_cases.description: Create a tool name, description, parameter schema, return format, and error guidance that an agent can use reliably.

use_cases.title: Reduce Tool Confusion
use_cases.description: Review an existing tool collection and identify overlapping tools that should be merged, renamed, or separated.

use_cases.title: Evaluate MCP Tool Interfaces
use_cases.description: Check whether MCP tool names, descriptions, and usage guidance are clear enough for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.

prompt_templates.title: Draft a Tool Description
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a clear agent tool description for this tool: [tool purpose]. Include when to use it, inputs, returns, and error cases.

prompt_templates.title: Review Tool Clarity
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this tool specification for ambiguity, missing parameter details, unclear returns, and weak error guidance: [tool specification].

prompt_templates.title: Consolidate a Tool Set
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze this tool collection and recommend which tools to merge, keep separate, rename, or remove: [tool list and workflows].

prompt_templates.title: Design an Agent Tool Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an agent tool architecture for [domain]. Include tool boundaries, naming conventions, response formats, safety controls, and evaluation criteria.

output_examples.input: Review a customer lookup tool that only says search customers.
output_examples.output: The response identifies missing usage triggers, unclear query format, missing return fields, and absent recovery guidance. It proposes a clearer customer retrieval contract.

output_examples.input: Evaluate ten tools used by a scheduling assistant.
output_examples.output: The response groups tools by workflow, highlights overlapping availability and booking actions, and recommends a smaller set with clearer responsibilities.

output_examples.input: Create MCP naming guidance for a mixed GitHub and database tool set.
output_examples.output: The response recommends fully qualified tool references, consistent verb-noun names, and examples that distinguish repository actions from database actions.

best_practices: Define each tool by what it does, when to use it, what inputs it accepts, and what it returns.
Consolidate sequential workflow steps when one tool can complete the user goal more reliably.
Document error cases with specific recovery guidance that an agent can act on.
anti_patterns: Do not use vague descriptions such as search the database without scope or return details.
Do not expose broad command execution without sandboxing, permission limits, and audit controls.
Do not create many overlapping tools when one clear workflow tool would reduce selection errors.

faq.question: What does this skill help me design?
faq.answer: It helps design AI agent tools, MCP interfaces, tool descriptions, schemas, response formats, and error messages.

faq.question: Can it generate production-ready tool code?
faq.answer: It can guide structure and provide examples, but production code still needs security review, tests, and integration work.

faq.question: Why does it recommend tool consolidation?
faq.answer: Consolidation reduces tool selection ambiguity when several tools represent steps in one workflow.

faq.question: Is the command execution guidance safe?
faq.answer: It is safe only when implemented with strong sandboxing, allowlists, resource limits, and sensitive data isolation.

faq.question: Does it support Claude Code and Codex workflows?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance applies to Claude, Codex, Claude Code, and other agents that choose tools from descriptions.

faq.question: What should I test after using this skill?
faq.answer: Test whether agents choose the right tool, pass valid parameters, interpret results correctly, and recover from errors.
